title: Pain and sedation in pediatrics
executiveSummary: Evaluate pain using age-validated scales (FLACC, VAS, pediatric CPOT), treat using a multimodal approach, and minimize unnecessary sedation.
body:
## Steps to perform

1. Assess pain systematically at the beginning of each shift and both before and after procedures.
2. Select a validated scale according to age and clinical situation: FLACC (0–7 years), VAS (> 6–7 years), COMFORT-B/CPOT (ICU/intubated), NIPS/PIPP (neonates).
3. Record the pain/sedation score in the medical record along with the time and clinical context.
4. Apply non-pharmacological measures: therapeutic containment involving a family member, breastfeeding or oral sucrose in infants, distraction, a quiet environment, and clustering care activities.
5. Administer pharmacological analgesia according to prescription and severity (paracetamol/ibuprofen for mild pain; titrated opioids for moderate-to-severe pain).
6. Administer analgesia prior to painful procedures when indicated (do not wait for peak pain intensity).
7. In the ICU, reevaluate sedation using an adapted scale (RASS); prioritize analgesia over sedation alone (PADIS).
8. Monitor for adverse effects: hypotension, bradypnea, respiratory depression associated with opioids/benzodiazepines, and nausea.
9. Involve the family in pain management and explain the analgesia plan.
10. Notify immediately in case of bradypnea, hypotension, oversedation, or uncontrolled pain despite treatment.

## Scientific rationale

### Principles

- Pain is frequently undertreated in pediatrics.
- Systematically evaluate pain every shift and before/after procedures.
- Combine pharmacological and non-pharmacological interventions.

### Assessment scales

| Age / situation | Scale |
| --- | --- |
| 0–7 years (preverbal) | FLACC (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability) |
| > 6–7 years (cooperative) | VAS (Visual Analog Scale 0–10) |
| ICU / Intubated | COMFORT-B / adapted CPOT |
| Neonate | NIPS / PIPP |

### Non-pharmacological interventions

- Therapeutic containment involving a family member.
- Breastfeeding or oral sucrose in infants for mild procedural pain.
- Distraction, music, and play.
- Maintaining a quiet environment and clustering care activities.

### Pharmacological analgesia (according to prescription)

| Situation | Common options |
| --- | --- |
| Mild pain | Paracetamol, oral/IV ibuprofen |
| Moderate-to-severe pain | Titrated opioids (morphine, fentanyl) |
| Brief procedure | Sedation with ketamine, propofol, or midazolam according to protocol |

### Sedation in the ICU

- Goal: a calm but assessable patient (avoid oversedation).
- Reevaluate sedation using a dedicated scale (adapted RASS).
- Monitor closely for respiratory depression associated with opioids and benzodiazepines.
- PADIS (pediatric adaptation of SCCM guidelines): prioritize analgesia over sedation alone.

### Nursing role

- Record the pain/sedation scale score during each shift.
- Administer analgesia prior to painful procedures when indicated.
- Monitor for adverse effects: hypotension, bradypnea, and nausea.
- Involve the family in pediatric pain management.

bibliography.citation: Devlin JW, et al. PADIS Guidelines — adaptación a población pediatric en UTI.
American Academy of Pediatrics. Prevention and Management of Procedural Pain in the Newborn. Pediatrics. 2016.
Society of Critical Care Medicine — PADIS Guidelines.
SATI — Guías de práctica clínica para prevención y manejo del dolor (traducción oficial español).
